description: Maps the value of an input variable to an output using a lookup table. You set value to a variable reference (e.g. {{ Referrer }}) and options.mapping to input→output pairs. When the input variable's value matches an entry, the variable returns the corresponding output. options.default_value when no match is found (optional). Useful for mapping campaign IDs, product IDs, referrer URLs to categories. Available from 16.33.0.

when_to_use: Map referrer URLs to campaign or source labels (e.g. https://promo.example.com/ → 'big promo')
Map campaign IDs or product IDs to human-readable names
Categorise or normalise values from another variable (string, number, or boolean)
Create conditional outputs based on the value of a built-in or custom variable
common_use_cases: Referrer mapping: value {{ Referrer }}, mapping → help center, blog, big promo
Product ID → product category or name
URL path or host → site section or environment
best_practices: value must be a variable reference in the form {{ Variable Name }}
Input variable value is converted to string for comparison; use a variable with string, number, or boolean value
Input and output in mapping are case-sensitive; empty string is allowed
If multiple mapping inputs match, the output for the last match is returned
Set options.default_value when no match is found, or the variable returns undefined

create_variable.description: Create a lookup table variable. Set value to the input variable reference (e.g. {{ Referrer }}), and options.mapping to the list of input→output pairs. Optionally set options.default_value.

variable_type.description: Must be 'lookup_table' for lookup table variables

value.description: Variable reference for the input variable (API: value). Must match pattern {{ Variable Name }}, e.g. {{ Referrer }}.

options.description: Object with mapping (array of { input, output }) and optionally default_value (string or variable UUID).

value.description: Variable reference used as input (API: value). Must be in the form {{ Variable Name }}, e.g. {{ Referrer }}. The variable's value is compared against options.mapping inputs.
value.recommendation: Use the exact variable name as in Tag Manager; the reference is case-sensitive.

options.description: Lookup table options (API: options). Must include mapping; optionally default_value.

mapping.description: Lookup table mapping (API: options.mapping). Array of input→output pairs. Each item: { input: string or variable reference, output: string or variable reference }. Comparison is case-sensitive.

items.input: Value to match against the input variable's value
items.output: Value returned when input matches (can be string or variable reference)

mapping.recommendation: Order matters: if input matches multiple rows, the last match wins.

default_value.description: Returned when input variable does not match any mapping input (API: options.default_value). Can be null, a static string, or a variable UUID.
default_value.recommendation: Set when you want a fallback instead of undefined.

options.recommendation: Ensure the variable referenced in value exists. Use exact strings for mapping input/output; comparison is case-sensitive.

create_referrer_example.description: Create a lookup table variable that maps referrer URL to a category label

update_example.description: Update lookup table mapping or default value

common_mistakes: Using a variable UUID for value instead of the variable reference {{ Variable Name }}
Wrong or misspelled variable name in value so the lookup never receives the expected input
Assuming case-insensitive comparison—input and output are case-sensitive
Expecting first match to win—when multiple mapping inputs match, the last match wins
Forgetting a default_value and getting undefined when nothing matches
Creating a circular dependency between variables

always_undefined_or_default.problem: Variable always returns undefined or the default value
solutions: Verify value is a valid variable reference in the form {{ Variable Name }} and that variable exists
Check that options.mapping input strings exactly match the input variable's value (case and spacing)
Remember input variable value is converted to string before comparison

wrong_output_for_match.problem: Getting a different output than expected for a match
solutions: If multiple rows could match, the last matching row wins; reorder or make inputs more specific
Confirm mapping input/output strings are exactly as intended (case-sensitive)
